name: Maricopa County, Arizona
tagline: The nation's 4th-largest county and the engine of the Phoenix metro: ~4.5M residents served across 50+ departments, with technology run centrally by the Office of Enterprise Technology (now branded Enterprise Technology & Innovation).
overview: Maricopa County is the most populous county in Arizona and the fourth most populous in the United States, with roughly 4.5 million residents across the Phoenix metro area. It is a large, multi-department government — more than 50 departments and over 13,000 employees — spanning public safety, courts, elections, public health, transportation, and environmental services. Central technology is delivered by the Office of Enterprise Technology (OET), which the County now publicly brands as Enterprise Technology & Innovation (ETI); it is the County's central enterprise technology provider, supplying infrastructure, application support, information security, GIS, and IT advisory services to the operating departments. For a Dell SLG seller, Maricopa is best understood as a single very large local-government account where the spending decisions are concentrated: OET/ETI shapes the technology direction, but contracts are awarded centrally through the Office of Procurement Services and must be approved by the elected Board of Supervisors. Public-safety functions (Sheriff, County Attorney, detention, courts) dominate the budget, and elections technology is an unusually high-profile workload given the County's national visibility.

budget.intro: Maricopa County adopted a FY2026 budget of roughly $3.9 billion. Public safety is the single largest claim on it (about half), and a large share of the General Fund is consumed by state-mandated costs — so the discretionary, technology-addressable pool is a fraction of the headline number. The County slightly reduced its primary property tax rate, signaling a cost-discipline posture rather than expansion.

stats.label: FY2026 adopted budget
stats.note: Board of Supervisors-adopted total all-funds budget

stats.label: Public safety share
stats.note: Largest budget category — Sheriff, courts, detention, County Attorney

stats.label: Primary property tax rate
stats.note: FY2026; a ~0.37% reduction from the prior year

notes: Nearly 20% of the General Fund operating budget goes to state-mandated costs, narrowing the discretionary pool.
OET/ETI is a central service provider — its IT spend is funded across the departments it serves rather than as a single visible line; map demand to the departments, not just the CIO.
Capital projects in the FY2026 plan include a new elections tabulation center (opening Summer 2027) and office-space consolidation projected to avoid up to $250M in cost.

initiatives.name: AI governance and adoption
initiatives.summary: OET/ETI has stood up a countywide AI usage policy, end-user training, and an AI subcommittee, and is adopting chatbots and productivity aids while managing security and data-privacy risk. An AI Governance Report (Oct 2025) tracks corrective actions. Lead with governed, secure AI infrastructure rather than point tools.

initiatives.name: Infrastructure, cloud and resilience modernization
initiatives.summary: OET's CTO function owns enterprise architecture, disaster recovery, business continuity, cloud services, and public-safety radio. This is the natural home for ISG (compute/storage), hybrid-cloud, and DR/BCP refresh conversations.

initiatives.name: Elections technology and the new Tabulation & Election Center
initiatives.summary: A new ~260,000-sq-ft state-of-the-art Tabulation and Election Center is under construction (groundbreaking 2025; opening Summer 2027, operational for the 2028 cycle), set to more than double tabulation capacity. A high-visibility, security-sensitive workload with infrastructure and endpoint implications.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity maturity
initiatives.summary: Under a CISO appointed in late 2024, the County continues to harden information security across 50+ departments — endpoint, identity, and data protection are recurring themes given the County's threat profile and elections exposure.

procurement.intro: Maricopa County buys under its own Procurement Code (adopted under A.R.S. 41-2501) through the Office of Procurement Services. The defining feature for a seller: contracts and contract changes must be approved by the elected Board of Supervisors. Cooperative purchasing is explicitly authorized, so confirm which vehicle gives Dell the cleanest, pre-competed path before pursuing a one-off solicitation.

vehicles.name: Maricopa County Office of Procurement Services
vehicles.detail: The central buying authority; runs solicitations and administers contracts under the County Procurement Code. All awards route to the Board of Supervisors for approval.

vehicles.name: Board of Supervisors approval
vehicles.detail: Required for procurement agreements and any change to contract terms — the formal decision gate. Time deals to the Board calendar and identify the sponsoring department.

vehicles.name: Cooperative contracts
vehicles.detail: The Procurement Code authorizes cooperative purchasing with other public procurement units and external entities (e.g., national co-ops, Arizona state contracts). Often the fastest pre-competed path onto the County.

powerStructure.name: Jen Pokorski
powerStructure.title: County Manager
powerStructure.note: Appointed April 2023; oversees 40+ departments and executes the Board of Supervisors' priorities. The top of the operational chain above OET/ETI.

powerStructure.name: Richard McHattie
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer, Office of Enterprise Technology
powerStructure.note: Leads OET/ETI and a team of 200+ supporting 50+ departments and 13,000+ employees. The primary technology relationship for the account.

powerStructure.name: Shea McGrew
powerStructure.title: Chief Technology Officer, OET
powerStructure.note: Owns enterprise architecture, disaster recovery, business continuity, cloud, and wireless/public-safety radio; previously Director of Enterprise Infrastructure, Operations & Cloud. Key contact for ISG and cloud.

powerStructure.name: Seema Patel
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer
powerStructure.note: CISO since October 2024 (succeeding Lester Godsey); two decades-plus of County program experience. Owns security policy, posture, and the elections/threat-exposure conversation.

powerStructure.name: Erick Lao
powerStructure.title: Deputy CIO, Customer Engagement & Workspace
powerStructure.note: Leads desktop, customer service center, and service management — the buying center for client/endpoint and digital-workspace refresh.

news.title: Maricopa County adopts ~$3.9B FY2026 budget; trims property tax rate
news.impact: Cost-discipline posture with public safety dominating spend — frame technology buys around efficiency, consolidation, and mandated-workload support.

news.title: OET/ETI publishes AI Governance Report with countywide AI policy and subcommittee
news.impact: Signals a governed AI adoption path — an opening for secure, policy-aligned AI infrastructure rather than ungoverned point tools.

news.title: County breaks ground on new Tabulation and Election Center (opening Summer 2027)
news.impact: A multi-year, security-sensitive infrastructure build with compute, storage, and endpoint needs ahead of the 2028 cycle.

news.title: Seema Patel named County CISO, succeeding Lester Godsey
news.impact: New security leadership — rebuild the cyber relationship map and align to her priorities across the 50+ department footprint.

dellActions: Anchor the relationship on OET/ETI (McHattie, CIO; McGrew, CTO) as the central technology decision center, while mapping demand back to the large operating departments (Sheriff, Elections, Courts, Public Health) that fund it.
Lead ISG and hybrid-cloud conversations through the CTO's infrastructure, DR/business-continuity, and cloud remit — tie to the County's resilience and consolidation goals.
Engage the CISO (Patel) early on endpoint, identity, and data protection given the County's elevated threat and elections exposure; position security as a cross-department program, not a single buy.
Confirm Dell's cleanest pre-competed path — a cooperative contract or Arizona state vehicle — and align every pursuit to the Board of Supervisors approval calendar with a sponsoring department.
Track the new Tabulation and Election Center build (opening Summer 2027) as a discrete infrastructure and endpoint opportunity ahead of the 2028 election cycle.
